summary: DHH recounts the disastrous launch of 'It Doesn't Have To Be Crazy At Work,' where publisher Harper Business botched nearly every aspect of the release. Despite paying mid six figures for the rights, Harper printed only 14,000 copies, Amazon sold out in five days, and it took a full month to restock. The cover design was poorly executed, and Harper offered a parade of excuses including tariffs and paper shortages. The irony of a chaotic launch for a book about calm workplaces is not lost on DHH. He reflects on the bad assumptions they made trusting a major publisher and the lessons learned about verifying every detail yourself.
key_points: Harper Business printed only 14,000 copies despite knowing Amazon accounts for 70-90% of sales, allocating just 3,300 to Amazon
The planning department's incentives were broken: they were judged on not printing too many books, not on having enough to meet demand
Amazon sold out in 5 days and was out of stock for a month, killing all sales momentum during peak publicity
DHH and Jason assumed a major publisher paying top dollar would handle logistics competently, revealing dangerous assumptions built on past good experiences with Crown
The book missed all major bestseller lists not because of lack of demand but because there were literally no books to sell
money_quotes: It shouldn't have to be this crazy at work, especially when work is publishing a book called It Doesn't Have To Be Crazy At Work.
You had one job, Harper. One job. Print the books, get them to book stores.
The planning department is judged not on having enough books, but only if they print too many, and end up with unsold inventory.
What good is having a wonderful launch campaign, if you have no books to sell?
Harper spent less than $30,000 to print books, because their planning department didn't want to risk sitting with $10,000 worth of unsold inventory if the book should bomb.

When you work with people who know what they're doing, it's so easy to assume that this is totally normal. That everyone is going to be at this level, because that's the baseline you see. But it's not normal.
We went for the publisher who bid the highest, and we assumed this meant they had real skin in the game.
If you're not going to make the lists, let it be for a good reason like, not enough people buy your book, not that you don't have books for all the people who want to buy.
key_insight: A high bid from a major publisher doesn't guarantee competence — misaligned internal incentives and bureaucratic dysfunction can sabotage even the most promising launches, and trusting 'the professionals' without verification is a costly assumption.
